title: How to stay calm when you know you'll be stressed | Daniel Levitin | TED

transcript.chinese: 几年前，我闯入了自己的房子。
transcript.english: A few years ago, I broke into my own house.

transcript.chinese: 我刚开车回家，那是蒙特利尔严冬的午夜左右，我正在穿过城镇拜访我的朋友杰夫，前廊上的温度计读数为负 40 度——别费心去问那是摄氏还是华氏，负 40 是两个温标的交汇处——天气非常冷。
transcript.english: I had just driven home, it was around midnight in the dead of Montreal winter, I had been visiting my friend, Jeff, across town, and the thermometer on the front porch read minus 40 degrees -- and don't bother asking if that's Celsius or Fahrenheit, minus 40 is where the two scales meet -- it was very cold.

transcript.chinese: 当我站在前廊上摸索口袋时，我发现我没有带钥匙。
transcript.english: And as I stood on the front porch fumbling in my pockets, I found I didn't have my keys.

transcript.chinese: 事实上，我可以透过窗户看到它们，它们就躺在餐桌上我放它们的地方。
transcript.english: In fact, I could see them through the window, lying on the dining room table where I had left them.

transcript.chinese: 于是我赶紧跑过去，把其他所有的门窗都试了一遍，都锁得很紧。
transcript.english: So I quickly ran around and tried all the other doors and windows, and they were locked tight.

transcript.chinese: 我想过给锁匠打电话——至少我有手机，但在半夜，锁匠可能需要一段时间才能出现，而且天气很冷。
transcript.english: I thought about calling a locksmith -- at least I had my cellphone, but at midnight, it could take a while for a locksmith to show up, and it was cold.

transcript.chinese: 我不能回朋友杰夫家过夜，因为第二天早上我要乘早班飞机飞往欧洲，我需要拿护照和手提箱。
transcript.english: I couldn't go back to my friend Jeff's house for the night because I had an early flight to Europe the next morning, and I needed to get my passport and my suitcase.

transcript.chinese: 所以，在绝望和寒冷中，我找到了一块大石头，我打破了地下室的窗户，清除了玻璃碎片，我爬了进去，找到了一块纸板，把它贴在了开口上，想着早上在去机场的路上，我可以打电话给我的承包商，请他修理它。
transcript.english: So, desperate and freezing cold, I found a large rock and I broke through the basement window, cleared out the shards of glass, I crawled through, I found a piece of cardboard and taped it up over the opening, figuring that in the morning, on the way to the airport, I could call my contractor and ask him to fix it.

transcript.chinese: 这会很贵，但可能不会比半夜的锁匠贵，所以我想，在这种情况下，我还是出来吧。
transcript.english: This was going to be expensive, but probably no more expensive than a middle-of-the-night locksmith, so I figured, under the circumstances, I was coming out even.

transcript.chinese: 现在，我是一名经过培训的神经科学家，我对大脑在压力下的表现有一些了解。
transcript.english: Now, I'm a neuroscientist by training and I know a little bit about how the brain performs under stress.

transcript.chinese: 它会释放皮质醇，提高你的心率，调节肾上腺素水平，并影响你的思维。
transcript.english: It releases cortisol that raises your heart rate, it modulates adrenaline levels and it clouds your thinking.

transcript.chinese: 所以第二天早上，当我睡​​得太少醒来时，我担心窗户上的洞，脑子里记着我必须打电话给我的承包商，还有寒冷的气温，以及我即将在欧洲举行的会议，而且，你知道，我的大脑里充满了皮质醇，我的思想是阴暗的，但我不知道它是阴暗的，因为我的思想是阴暗的。
transcript.english: So the next morning, when I woke up on too little sleep, worrying about the hole in the window, and a mental note that I had to call my contractor, and the freezing temperatures, and the meetings I had upcoming in Europe, and, you know, with all the cortisol in my brain, my thinking was cloudy, but I didn't know it was cloudy because my thinking was cloudy.

transcript.chinese: （笑声）直到我到达机场值机柜台时，我才意识到我没有护照。
transcript.english: (Laughter) And it wasn't until I got to the airport check-in counter, that I realized I didn't have my passport.

transcript.chinese: （笑声）所以我在冰天雪地里跑回家，用了40分钟，拿了护照，跑回机场，我及时赶到了，但他们把我的座位让给了别人，所以我被困在飞机后部，靠近浴室的地方，在一个不能倾斜的座位上，在八小时的飞行中。
transcript.english: (Laughter) So I raced home in the snow and ice, 40 minutes, got my passport, raced back to the airport, I made it just in time, but they had given away my seat to someone else, so I got stuck in the back of the plane, next to the bathrooms, in a seat that wouldn't recline, on an eight-hour flight.

transcript.chinese: 嗯，在那八小时里我有很多时间思考，但没有睡觉。
transcript.english: Well, I had a lot of time to think during those eight hours and no sleep.

transcript.chinese: （笑声）我开始想知道，我可以做些什么，可以建立一些系统来防止不好的事情发生吗？
transcript.english: (Laughter) And I started wondering, are there things that I can do, systems that I can put into place, that will prevent bad things from happening?

transcript.chinese: 或者至少，如果发生不好的事情，将最大限度地降低其成为彻底灾难的可能性。
transcript.english: Or at least if bad things happen, will minimize the likelihood of it being a total catastrophe.

transcript.chinese: 所以我开始思考这个问题，但直到大约一个月后我的想法才具体化。
transcript.english: So I started thinking about that, but my thoughts didn't crystallize until about a month later.

transcript.chinese: 我正在和我的同事、诺贝尔奖获得者丹尼·卡尼曼共进晚餐，我有些尴尬地告诉他我打破了窗户，而且，你知道，忘记了我的护照，丹尼告诉我，他一直在练习一种叫做“前瞻性事后诸葛亮”的东西。
transcript.english: I was having dinner with my colleague, Danny Kahneman, the Nobel Prize winner, and I somewhat embarrassedly told him about having broken my window, and, you know, forgotten my passport, and Danny shared with me that he'd been practicing something called prospective hindsight.

transcript.chinese: （笑声）这是他从心理学家加里·克莱因（Gary Klein）那里得到的东西，几年前他曾写过相关文章，也称为“事前剖析”。
transcript.english: (Laughter) It's something that he had gotten from the psychologist Gary Klein, who had written about it a few years before, also called the pre-mortem.

transcript.chinese: 现在，你们都知道什么是尸检了。
transcript.english: Now, you all know what the postmortem is.

transcript.chinese: 每当发生灾难时，专家团队就会介入并试图找出问题所在，对吗？
transcript.english: Whenever there's a disaster, a team of experts come in and they try to figure out what went wrong, right?

transcript.chinese: 丹尼解释说，在事前剖析中，你会展望未来，尝试找出所有可能出错的事情，然后尝试找出可以采取哪些措施来防止这些事情发生，或将损失降到最低。
transcript.english: Well, in the pre-mortem, Danny explained, you look ahead and you try to figure out all the things that could go wrong, and then you try to figure out what you can do to prevent those things from happening, or to minimize the damage.

transcript.chinese: 所以今天我想和大家讨论的是我们可以通过事前分析的形式做的一些事情。
transcript.english: So what I want to talk to you about today are some of the things we can do in the form of a pre-mortem.

transcript.chinese: 其中有些是显而易见的，有些则不那么明显。
transcript.english: Some of them are obvious, some of them are not so obvious.

transcript.chinese: 我将从明显的开始。
transcript.english: I'll start with the obvious ones.

transcript.chinese: 在家中指定一个地方放置容易丢失的东西。
transcript.english: Around the home, designate a place for things that are easily lost.

transcript.chinese: 现在，这听起来像是常识，确实如此，但基于我们的空间记忆的工作方式，有很多科学支持这一点。
transcript.english: Now, this sounds like common sense, and it is, but there's a lot of science to back this up, based on the way our spatial memory works.

transcript.chinese: 大脑中有一个叫做海马体的结构，它经过数万年的进化，可以记录重要事物的位置——水井在哪里，鱼在哪里，果树在哪里，友方和敌方部落居住的地方。
transcript.english: There's a structure in the brain called the hippocampus, that evolved over tens of thousands of years, to keep track of the locations of important things -- where the well is, where fish can be found, that stand of fruit trees, where the friendly and enemy tribes live.

transcript.chinese: 海马体是伦敦出租车司机大脑中变大的部分。
transcript.english: The hippocampus is the part of the brain that in London taxicab drivers becomes enlarged.

transcript.chinese: 这是大脑中让松鼠找到坚果的部分。
transcript.english: It's the part of the brain that allows squirrels to find their nuts.

transcript.chinese: 如果你想知道，有人确实做了这个实验，他们切断了松鼠的嗅觉，但他们仍然可以找到坚果。
transcript.english: And if you're wondering, somebody actually did the experiment where they cut off the olfactory sense of the squirrels, and they could still find their nuts.

transcript.chinese: 他们没有使用嗅觉，而是使用海马体，这是大脑中用于寻找事物的精致进化机制。
transcript.english: They weren't using smell, they were using the hippocampus, this exquisitely evolved mechanism in the brain for finding things.

transcript.chinese: 但它对于那些不怎么移动的东西确实有好处，而对于那些会移动的东西则不太好。
transcript.english: But it's really good for things that don't move around much, not so good for things that move around.

transcript.chinese: 这就是我们丢失车钥匙、老花镜和护照的原因。
transcript.english: So this is why we lose car keys and reading glasses and passports.

transcript.chinese: 因此，在家里，为钥匙指定一个位置——门边的挂钩，也许是一个装饰性的碗。
transcript.english: So in the home, designate a spot for your keys -- a hook by the door, maybe a decorative bowl.

transcript.chinese: 为您的护照准备一个特定的抽屉。
transcript.english: For your passport, a particular drawer.

transcript.chinese: 为您的老花镜准备一张特殊的桌子。
transcript.english: For your reading glasses, a particular table.

transcript.chinese: 如果你指定一个地点并且小心谨慎，那么当你寻找时，你的东西就会一直在那里。
transcript.english: If you designate a spot and you're scrupulous about it, your things will always be there when you look for them.

transcript.chinese: 旅行呢？
transcript.english: What about travel?

transcript.chinese: 用手机拍摄信用卡、驾驶执照、护照的照片，然后将其邮寄给自己，以便将其存储在云端。
transcript.english: Take a cell phone picture of your credit cards, your driver's license, your passport, mail it to yourself so it's in the cloud.

transcript.chinese: 如果这些东西丢失或被盗，可以方便更换。
transcript.english: If these things are lost or stolen, you can facilitate replacement.

transcript.chinese: 现在这些是一些相当明显的事情。
transcript.english: Now these are some rather obvious things.

transcript.chinese: 请记住，当您面临压力时，大脑会释放皮质醇。
transcript.english: Remember, when you're under stress, the brain releases cortisol.

transcript.chinese: 皮质醇有毒，会导致思维混乱。
transcript.english: Cortisol is toxic, and it causes cloudy thinking.

transcript.chinese: 因此，事前剖析实践的一部分是要认识到，在压力下，你不会处于最佳状态，因此你应该建立适当的系统。
transcript.english: So part of the practice of the pre-mortem is to recognize that under stress you're not going to be at your best, and you should put systems in place.

transcript.chinese: 也许没有什么比您面临医疗决定时更有压力的情况了。
transcript.english: And there's perhaps no more stressful a situation than when you're confronted with a medical decision to make.

transcript.chinese: 在某些时候，我们所有人都将处于这样的境地，我们必须对我们或所爱之人的医疗保健的未来做出非常重要的决定，以帮助他们做出决定。
transcript.english: And at some point, all of us are going to be in that position, where we have to make a very important decision about the future of our medical care or that of a loved one, to help them with a decision.

transcript.chinese: 所以我想谈谈这个。
transcript.english: And so I want to talk about that.

transcript.chinese: 我要谈谈一种非常特殊的医疗状况。
transcript.english: And I'm going to talk about a very particular medical condition.

transcript.chinese: 但这代表了各种医疗决策，实际上也代表了金融决策和社会决策——你必须做出的任何类型的决策都会受益于对事实的理性评估。
transcript.english: But this stands as a proxy for all kinds of medical decision-making, and indeed for financial decision-making, and social decision-making -- any kind of decision you have to make that would benefit from a rational assessment of the facts.

transcript.chinese: 因此，假设您去看医生，医生说：“我刚刚拿回您的化验结果，您的胆固醇有点高。”现在，你们都知道高胆固醇与心血管疾病、心脏病、中风的风险增加有关。
transcript.english: So suppose you go to your doctor and the doctor says, "I just got your lab work back, your cholesterol's a little high." Now, you all know that high cholesterol is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, heart attack, stroke.

transcript.chinese: 所以你认为高胆固醇并不是最好的事情，所以医生说：“你知道，我想给你一种可以帮助你降低胆固醇的药物，他汀类药物。”您可能听说过他汀类药物，您知道它们是当今世界上最广泛使用的处方药之一，
transcript.english: And so you're thinking having high cholesterol isn't the best thing, and so the doctor says, "You know, I'd like to give you a drug that will help you lower your cholesterol, a statin." And you've probably heard of statins, you know that they're among the most widely prescribed drugs in the world today,

transcript.chinese: 您甚至可能认识服用它们的人。
transcript.english: you probably even know people who take them.

transcript.chinese: 所以你会想，“是啊！
transcript.english: And so you're thinking, "Yeah!

transcript.chinese: 给我他汀类药物。”但此时你应该问一个问题，一个你应该询问的统计数据，大多数医生不喜欢谈论它，制药公司更不喜欢谈论它。
transcript.english: Give me the statin." But there's a question you should ask at this point, a statistic you should ask for that most doctors don't like talking about, and pharmaceutical companies like talking about even less.

transcript.chinese: 这是为了需要治疗的人数。
transcript.english: It's for the number needed to treat.

transcript.chinese: 现在，这是什么，NNT？
transcript.english: Now, what is this, the NNT?

transcript.chinese: 这是指在一个人得到帮助之前需要服用药物或接受手术或任何医疗程序的人数。
transcript.english: It's the number of people that need to take a drug or undergo a surgery or any medical procedure before one person is helped.

transcript.chinese: 你在想，这是什么样的疯狂统计数据？
transcript.english: And you're thinking, what kind of crazy statistic is that?

transcript.chinese: 数量应该是一。
transcript.english: The number should be one.

transcript.chinese: 如果没有帮助，我的医生不会给我开药。
transcript.english: My doctor wouldn't prescribe something to me if it's not going to help.

transcript.chinese: 但实际上，医疗实践并非如此。
transcript.english: But actually, medical practice doesn't work that way.

transcript.chinese: 这不是医生的错，如果是任何人的错，那就是像我这样的科学家的错。
transcript.english: And it's not the doctor's fault, if it's anybody's fault, it's the fault of scientists like me.

transcript.chinese: 我们还没有很好地弄清楚潜在的机制。
transcript.english: We haven't figured out the underlying mechanisms well enough.

transcript.chinese: 但葛兰素史克估计 90% 的药物仅对 30% 至 50% 的人有效。
transcript.english: But GlaxoSmithKline estimates that 90 percent of the drugs work in only 30 to 50 percent of the people.

transcript.chinese: 那么最广泛使用的他汀类药物治疗所需的人数，您认为是多少？
transcript.english: So the number needed to treat for the most widely prescribed statin, what do you suppose it is?

transcript.chinese: 需要多少人服用才能使一个人得到帮助？
transcript.english: How many people have to take it before one person is helped?

transcript.chinese: 这是根据研究从业者 Jerome Groopman 和 Pamela Hartzband 的研究得出的，并得到 Bloomberg.com 的独立证实。
transcript.english: This is according to research by research practitioners Jerome Groopman and Pamela Hartzband, independently confirmed by Bloomberg.com.

transcript.chinese: 我自己查了一下这些数字。
transcript.english: I ran through the numbers myself.

transcript.chinese: 300 人必须服用该药物一年才能预防心脏病发作、中风或其他不良事件。
transcript.english: 300 people have to take the drug for a year before one heart attack, stroke or other adverse event is prevented.

transcript.chinese: 现在您可能会想，“好吧，降低胆固醇的机会只有三百分之一。
transcript.english: Now you're probably thinking, "Well, OK, one in 300 chance of lowering my cholesterol.

transcript.chinese: 为什么不呢，医生？
transcript.english: Why not, doc?

transcript.chinese: 无论如何，给我处方吧。”但此时您应该询问另一个统计数据，即“告诉我副作用”。正确的？
transcript.english: Give me the prescription anyway." But you should ask at this point for another statistic, and that is, "Tell me about the side effects." Right?

transcript.chinese: 因此，对于这种特殊的药物，百分之五的患者会出现副作用。
transcript.english: So for this particular drug, the side effects occur in five percent of the patients.

transcript.chinese: 其中包括可怕的事情——使人衰弱的肌肉和关节疼痛、胃肠道不适——但现在你在想，“百分之五，这种情况不太可能发生在我身上，我仍然会服用药物。”但等一下。
transcript.english: And they include terrible things -- debilitating muscle and joint pain, gastrointestinal distress -- but now you're thinking, "Five percent, not very likely it's going to happen to me, I'll still take the drug." But wait a minute.

transcript.chinese: 请记住，在压力下，您的思维不会清晰。
transcript.english: Remember under stress you're not thinking clearly.

transcript.chinese: 所以提前考虑一下你将如何解决这个问题，这样你就不必当场制造推理链。
transcript.english: So think about how you're going to work through this ahead of time, so you don't have to manufacture the chain of reasoning on the spot.

transcript.chinese: 300人服药，对吗？
transcript.english: 300 people take the drug, right?

transcript.chinese: 一个人得到了帮助，这 300 个人中有百分之五有副作用，那就是 15 人。
transcript.english: One person's helped, five percent of those 300 have side effects, that's 15 people.

transcript.chinese: 您受到该药物伤害的可能性是该药物给您带来帮助的可能性的 15 倍。
transcript.english: You're 15 times more likely to be harmed by the drug than you are to be helped by the drug.

transcript.chinese: 现在，我并不是说你是否应该服用他汀类药物。
transcript.english: Now, I'm not saying whether you should take the statin or not.

transcript.chinese: 我只是说你应该和你的医生谈谈。
transcript.english: I'm just saying you should have this conversation with your doctor.

transcript.chinese: 医学伦理要求这样做，这是知情同意原则的一部分。
transcript.english: Medical ethics requires it, it's part of the principle of informed consent.

transcript.chinese: 您有权访问此类信息以开始讨论您是否愿意承担风险。
transcript.english: You have the right to have access to this kind of information to begin the conversation about whether you want to take the risks or not.

transcript.chinese: 现在你可能会想，我凭空捏造这个数字是为了达到震撼的效果，但事实上这是相当典型的，这个数字需要处理。
transcript.english: Now you might be thinking I've pulled this number out of the air for shock value, but in fact it's rather typical, this number needed to treat.

transcript.chinese: 对于 50 岁以上男性进行最广泛的手术——前列腺癌切除术，需要治疗的人数为 49 人。
transcript.english: For the most widely performed surgery on men over the age of 50, removal of the prostate for cancer, the number needed to treat is 49.

transcript.chinese: 是的，每一位受帮助的人就要做 49 台手术。
transcript.english: That's right, 49 surgeries are done for every one person who's helped.

transcript.chinese: 50% 的患者都会出现这种情况的副作用。
transcript.english: And the side effects in that case occur in 50 percent of the patients.

transcript.chinese: 它们包括阳痿、勃起功能障碍、尿失禁、直肠撕裂、大便失禁。
transcript.english: They include impotence, erectile dysfunction, urinary incontinence, rectal tearing, fecal incontinence.

transcript.chinese: 如果你幸运的话，并且你是拥有这些的 50% 的人之一，那么它们只能持续一两年。
transcript.english: And if you're lucky, and you're one of the 50 percent who has these, they'll only last for a year or two.

transcript.chinese: 因此，事前剖析的目的是提前思考你可能会提出的问题，从而推动对话的进展。
transcript.english: So the idea of the pre-mortem is to think ahead of time to the questions that you might be able to ask that will push the conversation forward.

transcript.chinese: 您不想在现场制造所有这些。
transcript.english: You don't want to have to manufacture all of this on the spot.

transcript.chinese: 您还想考虑生活质量等问题。
transcript.english: And you also want to think about things like quality of life.

transcript.chinese: 因为你经常有一个选择，你想要一个没有痛苦的较短的生命，还是一个在最后可能会遭受巨大痛苦的较长的生命？
transcript.english: Because you have a choice oftentimes, do you I want a shorter life that's pain-free, or a longer life that might have a great deal of pain towards the end?

transcript.chinese: 这些是现在需要与您的家人和亲人谈论和思考的事情。
transcript.english: These are things to talk about and think about now, with your family and your loved ones.

transcript.chinese: 你可能会一时冲动而改变主意，但至少你已经练习过这种思维方式。
transcript.english: You might change your mind in the heat of the moment, but at least you're practiced with this kind of thinking.

transcript.chinese: 请记住，我们的大脑在压力下会释放皮质醇，而此时发生的事情之一就是整个系统关闭。
transcript.english: Remember, our brain under stress releases cortisol, and one of the things that happens at that moment is a whole bunch on systems shut down.

transcript.chinese: 这有一个进化的原因。
transcript.english: There's an evolutionary reason for this.

transcript.chinese: 与捕食者面对面，你不需要你的消化系统，你的性欲，或者你的免疫系统，因为如果你的身体在这些东西上消耗新陈代谢而你不迅速反应，你可能会成为狮子的午餐，然后这些东西都不重要了。
transcript.english: Face-to-face with a predator, you don't need your digestive system, or your libido, or your immune system, because if you're body is expending metabolism on those things and you don't react quickly, you might become the lion's lunch, and then none of those things matter.

transcript.chinese: 不幸的是，正如丹尼·卡尼曼（Danny Kahneman）和他的同事们所表明的那样，在压力时期，理性、逻辑思维就被抛之脑后了。
transcript.english: Unfortunately, one of the things that goes out the window during those times of stress is rational, logical thinking, as Danny Kahneman and his colleagues have shown.

transcript.chinese: 因此，我们需要训练自己提前思考此类情况。
transcript.english: So we need to train ourselves to think ahead to these kinds of situations.

transcript.chinese: 我认为这里重要的一点是认识到我们所有人都有缺陷。
transcript.english: I think the important point here is recognizing that all of us are flawed.

transcript.chinese: 我们都会时不时地失败。
transcript.english: We all are going to fail now and then.

transcript.chinese: 这个想法是提前考虑这些故障可能是什么，建立有助于最小化损害的系统，或者从一开始就防止不好的事情发生。
transcript.english: The idea is to think ahead to what those failures might be, to put systems in place that will help minimize the damage, or to prevent the bad things from happening in the first place.

transcript.chinese: 回到蒙特利尔那个下雪的夜晚，当我旅行回来时，我让承包商在门旁边安装了一把密码锁，里面有一把前门的钥匙，这是一个容易记住的密码。
transcript.english: Getting back to that snowy night in Montreal, when I got back from my trip, I had my contractor install a combination lock next to the door, with a key to the front door in it, an easy to remember combination.

transcript.chinese: 我不得不承认，我还有成堆的邮件没有分类，还有成堆的电子邮件没有浏览。
transcript.english: And I have to admit, I still have piles of mail that haven't been sorted, and piles of emails that I haven't gone through.

transcript.chinese: 所以我还没有完全组织起来，但我认为组织是一个渐进的过程，而且我正在实现这一目标。
transcript.english: So I'm not completely organized, but I see organization as a gradual process, and I'm getting there.

transcript.chinese: 非常感谢。
transcript.english: Thank you very much.

transcript.chinese: （掌声）
transcript.english: (Applause)
